name: qa-reasoning-queries
description: 8 questions designed to stress-test reasoning quality across design patterns. Categories: multi-step math, logical deduction, factual synthesis, claim verification, sequential planning, and open-ended analysis.

user.content: A train travels at 120 km/h for 2.5 hours, then slows to 80 km/h for the next 1 hour 45 minutes. What is the total distance covered? Then, if a second train needs to cover the same total distance in exactly 3 hours, what constant speed must it maintain?

expectations.ground_truth: Leg 1: 120 × 2.5 = 300 km. Leg 2: 80 × 1.75 = 140 km. Total: 440 km. Required speed: 440 / 3 ≈ 146.7 km/h.

user.content: A store sells a jacket at 40% markup over cost. During a sale, it offers a 25% discount on the marked price. If the final sale price is $90, what was the original cost price? What is the store's actual profit or loss percentage on the cost?

All mammals are warm-blooded. Some warm-blooded animals lay eggs. Dolphins are mammals. Platypuses lay eggs. (1) Are dolphins warm-blooded? Explain. (2) Does "dolphins are mammals" imply dolphins lay eggs? Why or why not? (3) What is the minimum additional fact needed to conclude that platypuses are mammals?

expectations.ground_truth: (1) Yes — dolphins are mammals, all mammals are warm-blooded, so dolphins are warm-blooded (modus ponens). (2) No — "some warm-blooded lay eggs" does not imply all do; dolphins may or may not (requires additional fact). (3) Need: "platypuses are warm-blooded AND all egg-laying warm-blooded animals are mammals" — or simply "platypuses are mammals" directly.

user.content: Evaluate the following claim: "If a number is divisible by 6, it must be divisible by both 2 and 3. Therefore, if a number is divisible by 2 and 3, it must be divisible by 6." Is the claim correct? Provide a proof or a counterexample.

expectations.ground_truth: The claim is correct. 6 = 2 × 3 (coprime factors). If n divisible by 2 and 3, then by lcm(2,3) = 6. No counterexample exists — the biconditional holds because 2 and 3 are coprime.

user.content: Compare the computational complexity of binary search and linear search. For a sorted list of 1 million elements: how many comparisons does each require in the worst case? For which list sizes does linear search outperform binary search, if any?

expectations.ground_truth: Linear search: up to 1,000,000 comparisons (O(n)). Binary search: up to log2(1,000,000) ≈ 20 comparisons (O(log n)). Binary search requires sorted data and random access. For very small lists (n ≤ ~8) or unsorted data, linear search may be preferable due to lower overhead.

user.content: A company has 3 servers. Server A handles 50% of requests with a 2% error rate. Server B handles 30% with a 5% error rate. Server C handles 20% with a 1% error rate. What is the overall error rate? If a randomly chosen request errored, what is the probability it came from Server B?

You need to bake a layered cake. The steps are: mix batter (20 min), bake layer 1 (35 min), bake layer 2 (35 min), cool each layer (45 min each, can be done in parallel), make frosting (15 min), assemble (10 min). The oven fits only one layer at a time. What is the minimum total time? Show the critical path.

expectations.ground_truth: Critical path: mix (20) → bake L1 (35) → bake L2 (35) → cool L1+L2 in parallel (45) + frosting (15, overlaps cooling) → assemble (10). Total: 20 + 35 + 35 + 45 + 10 = 145 minutes. Frosting fits inside the 45-min cooling window so it does not add to critical path.

user.content: Explain why quicksort has an average case of O(n log n) but a worst case of O(n²). Under what concrete conditions does the worst case occur? What pivot selection strategy best mitigates this, and why?

expectations.ground_truth: Average: each partition splits roughly in half → recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n). Worst case: pivot always chosen as min/max → one partition of size n-1 → O(n²). Occurs with sorted/ reverse-sorted input + first/last pivot. Mitigation: median-of-3 or random pivot selection reduces worst-case probability to near zero.
